answers: Redaction Playback: on authorized replay, substitute each redacted field with a deterministic, hash-derived synthetic placeholder that preserves length and character class, allowing developers to safely inspect the full trace and debug agent decisions without ever exposing the original secrets.

**Permissioned Replay Decryption**: the redaction filter encrypts each stripped field under a separate key per access role (e.g., developer, auditor, support) and stores the set of ciphertexts with the trace; during authorized replay, the viewer’s session key decrypts only the fields their role is permitted to see, exposing the minimum necessary context while keeping everything else permanently redacted.
Redaction Replay Vault: replay of redacted fields requires role-based authorization, mandatory audit trail entry (who/when/why), and anomaly-based rate limiting.

Trace Dependencies: extend the run timeline by enriching each span with backward links to the upstream spans that generated its input messages, displayed as clickable dependency edges so developers can walk the exact data-flow chain.

Quorum-gated Replay: sensitive fields are captured as reversible salted hashes, but reversing a trace requires an M-of-N threshold of operator approvals that unlock a one-time decryption key, with every approval logged immutably for audit.

answers: Divergent Step Context Snapshot: When trace diff pinpoints the first step with differing outputs, automatically capture and attach the full pre-step execution state (serialized agent memory, tool call results, and LLM prompt) from both runs, enabling side-by-side inspection of the exact inputs that caused the divergence.

Cascade Analysis: after Trace Diff identifies the first step with divergent output, the tool continues comparing all subsequent steps, classifying each difference as a cascaded divergence (the step’s inputs changed because of the earlier divergence) or an independent divergence (inputs identical but output differs), then renders a propagation tree so the team sees exactly how the initial split rippled through the multi-agent run.

Contextual Diff: pinpoint the first diverging step and then diff the full input transcript (preceding messages, tool results) between the two runs, highlighting the exact differing payload that caused the output deviation.

Root-Cause Diff: after highlighting the first step whose output diverges between two runs, the tool automatically walks backward through each step’s input provenance to find the earliest upstream artifact (a tool result, LLM response, memory entry, or prompt fragment) that differs, tags it as the root cause, and optionally renders a propagation path showing how that difference cascaded into the final divergence.

Contextual Diff Lens: for the first diverging step, automatically expand the trace to reveal the earliest upstream message (user input, tool output, or prior agent response) that differed between the two runs, rendering a side-by-side token-level diff of that message to pinpoint the root-cause variation.

answers: Credential Replay Audit: temporary, read-only injection of original secrets into trace spans within a short-lived debug session, logged immutably to an access ledger.

Policy-Gated Replay Decryption: each redacted field is stored as a ciphertext sealed to a set of role or attribute-based access policies; during an authorized replay, the trace viewer decrypts only the fields that satisfy the viewer’s current policy set, and every single decryption event is recorded in an append-only audit log, giving teams trustworthy, low-friction inspection with full replay accountability.

Authorized Replay Audit Trail: wrap the reversible hash lookup behind a policy engine that validates the requester’s role and justification, then logs each re-identification event to an append-only audit log with timestamp, identity, and trace ID.

Redaction Feedback Loop: store each redacted field with its classification rule and a reversible hash; after authorized replay, developers can mark false positives/negatives, and the system retrains the redaction filter to reduce the chance that critical context for agent decisions is incorrectly hidden in future runs.
Replay Vault: encrypt the reversible hash under a team-managed key, gate decryption with a time-limited, signed justification token, and record every replay attempt in an append-only ledger.

answers: Divergence Cascade: after the Trace Diff identifies the first step with a diverging output, automatically continue pairwise step-by-step comparison downstream, feeding each run’s actual outputs forward, and mark every subsequent step whose output differs from the baseline—building a propagation graph that reveals the full ripple effect of the initial divergence.

Semantic Diff: embed each step’s output into a dense vector and flag the first step where cosine distance exceeds a configurable threshold, ignoring non-semantic phrasing differences so developers focus on meaningful behavioral divergence.

Input Delta Isolation: once Trace Diff flags the first step with divergent output, the tool automatically diffs that step’s full input state (system prompt, tool schemas, retrieved document fragments, and the prefix of the conversation history) across the two runs and highlights the minimal token-level substring whose presence or value shifted the output, giving developers a clickable causal breadcrumb.

Divergence Root-Cause Backtrace: When Trace Diff flags the first step whose output diverges, the tool automatically compares the full upstream context—including the agent’s chain-of-thought traces, tool call arguments, and raw tool responses—from both runs, identifies the earliest token-level or field-level discrepancy that eventually caused the output split (even if the containing step’s final output appeared identical), and surfaces a one-line natural‑language explanation of the causal chain.

Semantic Divergence Threshold: for the first diverging step, compute an embedding similarity score between the two outputs; suppress the alert if the score exceeds a configurable threshold, so teams only investigate genuine semantic shifts, not superficial rephrasings.

answers: State Checkpoint Diff: automatically capture an immutable snapshot of the full agent context (system prompt, message history, retrieved documents, tool outputs) at each handoff, then let operators visually diff any two snapshots from the same run to instantly surface the exact contextual delta that caused a change in reasoning, enabling root-cause isolation and deterministic rollback to the pre-divergent state.

Claim-to-Source Trace: for any selected span of a final output, reconstruct and display the causal chain of agent handoffs, tool calls, and context snippets that directly contributed to it, with one-click replay of each step’s exact prompt and model response.

Custodial Trace Ledger: all agent steps, annotations, and redacted hashes are committed to an append-only, time-stamped log with Merkle integrity proofs; field-level payloads are encrypted under role-specific keys, so only authorized principals can reveal plaintext, and every decryption event (including redaction reversal) emits a tamper-resistant audit entry.

Hypothesis Check: developers annotate each agent handoff with their predicted next action; the trace diffs that prediction against what actually occurred, flagging mismatches to expose flawed mental models and drive durable understanding.

Intent-to-Outcome Delta: for each agent step, diff the expected behavior (from prompt) against the actual output and tool calls, flagging deviations (e.g., ignored directives, unauthorized actions) and computing the token budget wasted on off-policy reasoning.

answers: Fault Injection Handoff Harness: at each inter-agent message boundary, stochastically mangles payloads (injected latency, truncation, schema violations, contradictory instruction inserts) and measures the workflow’s survival rate, recovery path activation, and error propagation depth to expose brittle handoffs before production.

Handoff Sensitivity Profiler: for each inter-agent message type in the workflow, automatically generate a suite of plausible linguistic perturbations (paraphrases, slot omissions, polarity flips) and replay the downstream agent’s response; block the merge if the probability of the downstream agent selecting a different tool or action exceeds a configurable threshold, highlighting fragile handoff points that need prompt hardening or guardrails.

Scoped Replay Gate: replay recorded multi-agent traces in an isolated runtime that enforces declared per-agent tool permissions, data-access boundaries, and tenant isolation; block deployment if any call exceeds its scope, accesses cross-tenant data, or touches unlabelled PII.

Causal Probe: developers annotate a decision point in a workflow trace with a suspected cause (e.g., “Agent A’s reply in step 2 caused the switch to plan B”); the tool automatically generates minimal perturbations of that step’s context (reword, reorder, substitute an equivalent tool output) and re-executes only the downstream agents, then displays a before/after comparison of the outcome. The result exposes which dependencies actually flip the behavior, helping the team replace guesswork with an evidence-based mental model of agent interactions.

Cost Forecast Gate: simulates parallel agent runs using production-shaped input samples, projects daily token burn, p95 latency, and infrastructure cost, and blocks the pull request if any estimate exceeds the team’s quota or SLO.

answers: Confidence Gating: after repeating each nondeterministic test case N times, compute a 95% lower confidence bound on pass rate and block shipping if it falls below a required threshold.

**Semantic Flake Distillation:** After a flaky test is re-run N times, an LLM judge clusters the raw outputs into outcome categories (e.g., “intent match”, “stylistic variant”, “wrong tool”, “refusal”) and surfaces a **functional flake ratio**—the proportion of runs that crossed a meaningful correctness boundary—alongside the raw variance band, so teams can dismiss benign nondeterminism and build confidence around behavior that actually impacts the product.

Flake Detector + Sensitive Drift Audit: rerun each nondeterministic case with identical inputs across N trials, capture full execution context (seed, temperature, tool call arguments, environment variables) along with the standard variance band, and separately highlight any output field tagged as containing PII or credentials that changes value across runs—flagging it as a potential nondeterministic privacy leak with per-field histograms and audit snapshots.

Stability Gate: repeats nondeterministic cases until the 95% confidence interval for the success rate crosses a predefined stability threshold (e.g., >99%), then either auto-approves or blocks the deployment with a quantified risk report.

Confidence Budget Flake Detector: repeats nondeterministic workflow runs under a pre-set token budget and latency cap, employing early stopping when the variance band sits within an acceptable tolerance, then reports the achieved confidence interval together with the token expenditure and the throughput impact.

answers: Shadow Promotion Gate: a pre-merge pipeline that deploys the candidate multi-agent workflow as a shadow next to the stable version; it replays a curated corpus of production scenarios (Replay Harness) plus coverage-gap cases (Scenario Matrix), repeats nondeterministic runs to build a variance band (Flake Detector), then blocks promotion unless the new version’s task-success rate and output drift stay within a pre-defined reliability budget, providing a statistically grounded go/no-go before any live traffic is touched.

Diff Tracer: automatically aligns a failing replay trace against its baseline and highlights the earliest agent turn where the conversation diverges, showing the prompt and intermediate reasoning that produced the deviation so developers can instantly isolate the root cause.

Secure Replay Gate: replay recorded multi-agent traces with credential stubbing and instrumented tool-call allow-lists; repeat across non-deterministic runs to detect flaky policy violations, then block the release if any run leaks PII or invokes an unauthorized tool beyond a zero-tolerance threshold.

Cost Drift Budget: a pre-merge gate that replays a curated set of multi-agent workflow traces under the proposed change, computes the resulting token consumption and end-to-end latency delta, and blocks the merge if the projected per‑request cost or p95 latency exceeds the team’s shipping budget.

answers: **Variance Safety Gate**: Repeats nondeterministic workflow runs, measures output variance bands on critical assertions (correctness, tool call ordering, toxicity), and automatically blocks promotion if any band exceeds a pre‑calibrated safety margin, triggering a rollback to the last known‑good configuration.

Variance Threshold Gate: after repeating nondeterministic cases, compute a 95% confidence interval for each critical output metric and block the workflow from promotion if any interval’s lower bound drops below the minimum acceptable value, so teams only ship behaviors whose worst plausible variation still meets the quality bar.

Flake Drift Detector: establish per‑workflow‑step variance baselines from repeated runs, then block a prompt, model, or tool change that statistically significantly widens the variance band (e.g., via a two‑sample dispersion test with a confidence threshold).

Flake Absorption: uses repeated non-deterministic runs to cluster failure patterns by input features, then automatically generates new regression tests that lock down the discovered fragile subspaces, shrinking the variance band and building durable confidence through a feedback loop that converts flake mystery into covered edge cases.

**Adaptive Token-Budget Sampler:** repeats a flaky case until the output quality variance band’s 95% confidence interval stabilizes (change < 2%) or a per-case token cap is reached, then reports the final band and total tokens consumed.

answers: **Chaos Preflight**: injects a catalogue of operational faults (agent timeouts, tool schema violations, duplicate inter-agent messages) into the workflow’s execution graph and asserts that each perturbation results in either graceful recovery, a well-defined error signal, or a safe halt within a deadline—flagging any silent hang, partial output, or cascading state corruption.

**Idempotency Gate**: run the entire multi-agent workflow twice on the same trigger using different LLM random seeds; intercept and compare all external side-effects (API calls, database writes, emitted events) and block the release if the set or content of those mutations differs.
This catches nondeterministic drifts that could cause duplicate orders, inconsistent state, or retry-safety failures—building confidence that the workflow behaves repeatably where it matters before it hits production.

Least-Privilege Replay Gate: assign each tool and data source a minimum privilege level; replay recorded multi-agent workflows with agent identities mapped to trust tiers, then block the pipeline if any agent attempts a tool call or data access beyond its tier.

Belief Audit: after a multi-agent run, an evaluator model extracts each agent's implicit factual and normative assumptions from its reasoning trace and surfaces a catalog of unverified beliefs, letting developers inspect which hidden presumptions drove decisions before assumptions break in production.

Cost-Profiling Matrix: for every committed test-scenario path, execute at projected production throughput, measure tail-latency and total token consumption, and gate the workflow if any path exceeds the deployment cost/latency budget.

Feature Name: **Agent Chaos Sandbox**  
Mechanism: Simulate transient LLM API failures, network partitions, and agent crashes within a local multi-agent workflow harness that records state snapshots after each step. It then measures recovery rates, identifies missing rollback points, and flags conversation drift—giving teams a reproducible, data-driven confidence score before shipping.
Chaos Puppetry: inject synthetic failures and edge-case inputs into a recorded multi-agent trace, then replay step-by-step to observe recovery and handoff behavior.

Canary-Leak Detector: automatically injects unique, auditable canary values into each agent's private memory before a test run, then scans all inter-agent messages and outputs for those canaries to detect unauthorized data flows across trust boundaries, producing a timestamped evidence log for security review.

Counterfactual Conversation Replay: a debugger that records all inter-agent messages, lets developers pause and replace any agent’s output with a hand-crafted alternative, and instantly re-simulates the rest of the dialogue, highlighting differences in subsequent turns and final decisions to build an intuitive map of causal dependencies and failure modes.

Feature Name: Token-Latency Sandbox Mode

Mechanism: Injects a simulated LLM backend that returns synthetic responses with configurable token lengths and processing delays, running the full multi-agent workflow to measure aggregate token consumption, per-step latency, and throughput under concurrent loads—allowing teams to verify budgets and SLAs without real API costs.

answers: Agent Chaos Gate: a pre-merge check that replays a recorded multi-agent workflow trace with stubbed tool calls, randomly injecting transient faults (timeouts, malformed responses, 500s) and latency. The check fails the change if any agent’s retry loop exceeds a budget or if overall execution misses a wall-clock deadline, preventing unbounded loops and brittle failure handling from reaching production.

Release Confidence Gate: Combines pass/fail signals from prompt diff checks, scenario coverage gaps, flake variance bands, and replay baseline fidelity into a single weighted readiness score that blocks merge when below a configurable threshold, with drill-down to each signal.

Access Baseline Gate: replay committed test scenarios with an instrumented runtime that logs every tool call and inter-agent data transfer, then diff the observed access footprint against a per-agent least-privilege manifest; block the merge if any new external call, privilege escalation, or cross-boundary data flow appears.

Intent Trace Diff: Compare each agent’s chain-of-thought trace against developer-annotated expected reasoning steps; flag any divergence as a mismatch that prompts the team to explain why the expectation was wrong, then automatically crystallise the corrected understanding into a new pinned regression test.

Token-Latency Budget Guard: on pull request, replay a curated scenario suite against the modified workflow, instrument every LLM call to project per-invocation token consumption and p95 latency, and block the merge if the delta would violate the team’s pre-approved deployment budget or latency SLO.

answers: Prompt Drift Budget Gate: for each pinned regression case, compute a multi-axis drift score (semantic similarity, safety classifier delta, token cost, latency) between the edited prompt’s output and the baseline; block the merge if any axis exceeds a team-configured tolerance, with a drift report highlighting the specific cases and axes that crossed the budget.

Behavioral Similarity Gate: augment the prompt diff gate with a configurable semantic similarity threshold (e.g., BERTScore) per pinned case, blocking the merge if any case’s score drops below it, and present a ranked diff view of the most semantically shifted outputs.

Prompt Diff Gate with Data Leak Detection: block merge if the prompt diff introduces new sensitive data patterns (secrets, PII, or internal schema references) not present in the approved baseline, in addition to behavioral regression on pinned cases.

Prompt Regression Explanation: when the diff gate blocks a merge, it automatically produces a step-by-step reasoning trace diff—contrasting the original and edited prompt’s thought chains on each pinned case—to make the behavioral regression legible and guide a targeted fix.

Inter-Agent Prompt Diff Gate: block merge when a prompt edit in any agent changes behavior on a pinned regression case in that agent *or* in any downstream agent, catching cascading output shifts across the multi-agent workflow.

answers: Trace-Aware Replay: captures the full multi-agent execution trace (tool calls, intermediate messages, agent handoffs) during each replay and diffs them against the baseline trace, flagging behavioral regressions even when final outputs stay identical.

**Semantic Trace Diff**: The replay harness captures full agent execution traces (reasoning steps, tool calls, messages) and, on rerun, performs a semantic alignment between the new trace and the baseline using embedding-based step matching. It highlights behavioral changes—such as a different tool selected or a critical reasoning shift—while suppressing format-only noise, and provides a side-by-side annotator to accept intentional deltas and promote the new trace as the approved baseline in a single click.

answers: Semantic Drift Gate: for each pinned regression case, compute the cosine similarity between the baseline and proposed prompt’s output embeddings; block the merge if any case’s similarity drops below a configurable threshold, and attach the most divergent output excerpts to the gate failure.

Amplified Diff Gate: when a prompt edit triggers a regression-case behavioral change, the gate automatically synthesizes a minimal set of boundary and adversarial test inputs targeted at the semantic difference, then blocks the merge until those new cases pass (or are manually accepted as new baselines), producing a quantified confidence score on the change’s blast radius.

Prompt Security Regression Gate: extend the prompt diff gate with a pinned adversarial suite (injection, PII extraction, jailbreak cases); block merge if a prompt edit causes any previously passing security case to fail.

Prompt Diff Gate with Causal Trace-back: when a merge is blocked, the gate automatically identifies the minimal prompt edit responsible for each behavioral regression and generates a natural-language explanation of how that edit shifted the agent’s reasoning, giving developers a durable understanding of the prompt’s sensitivity.

Prompt Cost-Diff Gate: block a merge when an edited prompt changes behavior or exceeds token/latency budgets on any pinned regression case, surfacing per-case cost and latency deltas alongside behavioral diffs.

answers: Golden Path Regression Gate: every change to an agent prompt or tool wiring must re-execute a shared, curated suite of end‑to‑end conversations that exercise multiple agents. The merge is blocked unless all agent outputs match the expected golden responses within a defined similarity threshold; failures highlight the diverging agent and provide a side‑by‑side diff, giving the team an immediate rollback path (revert the change) and clear observability into which interaction broke.

Change Impact Replay: whenever a collaborator edits a prompt or tool wiring, automatically run a standardized test suite through the full multi-agent graph and surface a side-by-side diff of every agent’s output and the final result, so downstream breakage is visible before review.

Flow Guard: Agents declare a data sensitivity tier and owner; the orchestration layer blocks any tool or agent wiring that would pass sensitive output to a downstream agent whose owner lacks the required clearance tier; every blocked attempt is recorded in an immutable audit log for security review.

Explainback Merge: before a change to an agent, prompt, or tool wiring is merged, the author writes a concise rationale, and a reviewer must restate the change’s intent and expected downstream effects; the merge only completes when the author confirms the restatement is accurate, building shared understanding through reciprocal explanation and immediate feedback.

Cost-Aware Merge Gate: automatically calculate the projected per-turn token consumption of a merged workflow against the team’s token budget threshold, and require a cost owner’s approval if the change raises the cost beyond that limit.

answers: Ownership-Enforced Rollback: Rollback of an agent, prompt, or tool is only executable by its mapped owner (or a delegated backup), with every ownership change and rollback action recorded immutably in an audit log so that recovery keeps clear accountability.

Ownership Delegation: each owner can designate a time-bound deputy for an agent, prompt, or tool; all automated notifications and contact links automatically re-route to the deputy until the delegation expires, keeping responsibility unambiguous during absences.

Ownership-Aware Modification Gate: each agent, prompt, and tool is tagged with an owner; any edit to a component’s definition or logical wiring must be approved by that owner, and if the change introduces a new data flow between components owned by different people, it triggers a mandatory review by all downstream owners to confirm they accept the privacy/data exposure implications, with all approval/rejection events recorded in an immutable audit trail.

Adaptive Ownership Map: each ownership entry carries a responsiveness score initialized from the static assignment. When a question is routed to a person, the time-to-first-reply and whether the query is resolved feed back into the score via a lightweight reinforcement signal. Unresolved queries automatically escalate to the next wider role (e.g., team lead → channel). Entries whose scores drop below a threshold are flagged for review or reassignment. This closes the loop between routing decisions and real interaction data, continuously sharpening ownership accuracy and making responsibility boundaries learnable and durable.

Ownership Broadcast: When a component (agent, prompt, tool) is modified, the ownership map automatically pushes a notification to the owners of all downstream agents that consume its outputs, supplying the diff and a request to verify their agent’s behavior still aligns.

The review panel shows both the diff and the generated summary, letting owners approve or request adjustments without hunting for context. This merges ownership clarity with change review, reducing notification noise and making the right person aware instantly.

answers: Approved-Change Canary: upon approval, the edit is deployed to a small live-traffic slice; an automated safety gate compares task-success rate, agent loops, and tool-call errors against the stable baseline, and if degradation exceeds a preset threshold, it instantly reverts the change, freezes the affected traces, and pings the reviewer and author with a diff of the divergence.

**Behavior-Aware Review**: every change request to agent prompts or tool wiring automatically runs the modified workflow against a curated set of test conversations and surfaces a trace diff—showing added, removed, or altered agent responses and tool invocations—next to the config diff, so the reviewer can see the downstream impact before approving.

Change Review with Role-Based Quorum and Signed Audit Trail: require approvals from at least one engine owner and one security reviewer for modifications to agent prompts or tool wiring that access sensitive data or external APIs; each approval action, along with the exact diff and timestamp, is cryptographically signed and appended to an immutable, queryable audit log for non-repudiation and compliance review.

Explain-to-Approve: each proposed prompt or wiring edit requires the second approver to write a plain‑language rationale of how the change refines multi‑agent coordination; the rationale is versioned alongside the diff, turning every approval into a persistent, searchable explanation that builds durable team understanding.

**Change Review with Impact Blast Radius**: automatically flag which downstream agents, tools, or outputs could be affected by the proposed edit, and require the second approver to explicitly acknowledge the blast radius before the change lands.

answers: Bisection Rollback Safety: continuously monitor execution health (error rates, tool-call success, task outcomes) across all parallel workflow branches; on detecting a regression, automatically bisect the merge lineage of all contributors to pinpoint the breaking change, then roll back only that specific delta while preserving every other in-progress branch, and alert its owner.

Agent Contracts: versioned I/O schemas on each agent; when a developer changes an agent’s output schema, the tool automatically alerts all downstream agent owners, blocks pipeline execution, and requires their explicit approval before the change can land, preventing silent breakage during parallel development.

Data Ownership Review: require the designated owner of each data source or tool (as recorded in the Ownership Map) to approve any branch merge that alters an agent’s access to that resource; the approval decision and a mandatory justification are immutably logged for auditability, enforced by the version control system.

**Annotated Behavioral Review**: each proposed workflow change is automatically replayed against a shared suite of test scenarios; the tool highlights output deltas and requires the author to annotate each meaningful deviation with a short explanation. Reviewers then examine the diff side‑by‑side with those annotations, discussing and refining the rationale—turning every change into a durable, collective learning artifact that builds shared mental models of agent behavior.

**Cost-Impact Branch Previews**: each time a collaborator branches a shared multi-agent workflow, the tool simulates the modified graph, automatically profiling total token consumption, end-to-end latency, and throughput under realistic concurrency. These estimates are compared against the project’s token budget and deployment SLOs, and a merge is blocked if the branch would violate a hard constraint; team members see a side-by-side diff of cost/latency so they can jointly re-optimise before integrating.

answers:
**Immutable Capability Policy with Enforced Rollback Snapshots**  
A per-agent declarative policy file specifies exactly which tools, API calls, and data scopes are allowed. A sidecar proxy enforces these rules at runtime, logs every action to an append-only audit trail, and automatically captures a workflow state snapshot before each external write—enabling instant, automated rollback if any policy violation or unexpected side effect occurs.

**Policy-as-Code Guardrails**: A Git-tracked YAML manifest declaring each agent’s permitted tool calls, resource scopes, and inter-agent message targets; a central enforcement proxy blocks disallowed actions at runtime while a dry-run mode surfaces violations in logs without interrupting execution, letting teams iteratively tighten constraints.

Manifest-Driven Access Guard: A declarative manifest per agent specifies allowed actions, data bounds, and permitted outputs; a runtime enforcer validates every LLM-initiated action against it, blocking violations and recording immutable audit events.

**Feature Name:** Explainable Permission Guard with Policy Evolution Loop

**Mechanism:**  
The tool lets teams author allowed agent actions as declarative rules (e.g., “agents may only read from the `docs/` directory”). Before execution, any proposed action—like an API call or file access—is checked against these rules. If disallowed, the action is blocked and the tool immediately surfaces a structured natural-language explanation that links the specific action to the violated rule and its rationale (e.g., “The agent tried to write to `config.yaml`, which violates rule #3: *no agent may modify configuration files* because they control system integrity”). The team reviews this in a dashboard where they can either override with a recorded justification or refine the rule. Over time, the dashboard clusters violation patterns and suggests policy adjustments (e.g., “Agents frequently attempt to read from `logs/`; consider adding a read-only exception”). This creates a tight feedback loop that turns enforcement into a shared learning process, building durable, team-wide understanding of the multi-agent system’s boundaries.

**Permission Contract**: a JSON schema that defines allowed tool invocations, API calls, and cross-agent messages per agent, enforced by a sidecar proxy that intercepts and validates every action before execution.

answers: Revocation Rollback: automatically undo any side effects of an agent step whose capability is revoked mid-execution, using a recorded transaction log to reverse mutations and restore the system to its pre-step state.

Guarded Escalation: Pause a multi-agent run the moment an agent requests a capability not in its active grant, present the request with inferred risk context to a designated human reviewer, and upon approval issue a one-shot, tightly-scoped token that is automatically revoked after the step—while recording the decision for audit and future policy refinement.

Denial or timeout triggers live revocation of that step, and every approval/denial is recorded in a queryable audit trail with the approving identity, timestamp, and justification.

Shadow Grant Replay: replay a sample of recent production traces with the proposed capability enabled in shadow mode, surfacing a side-by-side diff of which agent actions would have been newly allowed or blocked, annotated with context from the trace, so the team can preview impact and build a durable mental model before enforcing.

Just-in-Time Permission Elevation Gate: when an agent attempts a tool call or data access that exceeds its baseline least-privilege token, the system pauses the workflow, sends a structured approval request (agent identity, target action, resource, and a brief LLM-generated justification) to a designated human reviewer via a configurable channel (e.g., Slack, webhook), and on explicit approval mints a single-use, tightly scoped, time-bounded (e.g., 60s) capability token limited to that exact call; scope automatically reverts afterward.

answers: Revertable Capability Window: every grant to an agent includes a brief time-to-live and an automatic pre-execution state snapshot; the orchestrator immediately rolls back any side effects (applying the snapshot) if the step fails its post-condition checks, is live-revoked, or the TTL expires, ensuring a safe, auditable undo path for every privileged action.

Pre-Authorization Impact Preview: a sandboxed dry-run of the agent’s next planned steps using the requested capability, rendered as a side-by-side diff of all projected API calls, data reads/writes, and state mutations for the human approver to inspect before granting.

Taint-Bound Capability Check: each agent’s planned action is intercepted, the data provenance (taint) of all inputs is resolved against the target capability’s allowed data sensitivity labels, and the call is blocked unless the flow satisfies a predefined policy—blocked attempts are logged with full context for audit.

Drift-to-Decision Feedback Loop: perpetually logs every capability invocation, measures divergence from the currently approved scoped grants against their recorded justifications, and surfaces evidence-backed suggestions to narrow over-privileged tokens; teams can accept a narrowing to instantly revoke the excess (even in running workflows), while rejected discrepancies are appended to the justification audit trail for future review, turning every runtime drift into a durable, auditable policy decision.

Cap-Spend Token: each agent invocation includes a short-lived, signed capability token listing allowed tools with per-tool invocation limits and a total token‑budget ceiling. An enforcement layer maintains a fast spend counter per run; it validates the token, atomically checks and decrements the counters, and immediately rejects any call that would exceed a limit—halting the step in-flight (live revocation) and logging the denial in a queryable audit trail.

answers: Justification Liveness Gate: Each grant recorded in the audit trail ties to an external justification artifact (e.g., change ticket, risk acceptance). Before any agent exercises the granted capability, the orchestrator queries that artifact’s current state—if it’s closed, expired, or superseded, the step is blocked, and a denial event is appended to the audit trail for post-hoc analysis, preventing “stale-approval” drift.

**Feature Name: Justification Drift Monitor**  
**Mechanism:** Continuously scans the grant audit trail and cross-references each approval’s recorded justification against live changes in policies, data classifications, or threat intelligence. Whenever a justification becomes stale (e.g., “approved for non‑PII data” but the dataset is now tagged as containing PII), the monitor flags the grant, notifies the original approver, and blocks reuse until a re‑approval with an updated justification is recorded—keeping the audit trail always aligned with current reality.

Grant Integrity Verifier: automatically cross-references the justification stored in each grant audit record with the agent’s actual tool calls and data accesses during execution, and appends a compliance mismatch flag directly into the queryable audit trail so reviewers see where intent and action diverged.

Policy-Anchored Justification Traceability: Each approval entry in the audit trail must link to a specific, versioned policy clause; the system automatically checks that the granted capability's scope and constraints are consistent with the cited clause's language, flagging any deviations and preventing future grants from referencing outdated or conflicting justifications.

Policy-Anchored Audit Trail: Each capability grant record is permanently linked to the precise policy rule version that authorized it, making the trail queryable to instantly identify all historical approvals affected by any policy change and automatically flag those that would now be non-compliant.

Feature Name: Agent Authorization Proxy  
Mechanism: A lightweight, per-agent sidecar that intercepts every tool call, data read/write, and inter-agent message. It enforces a centralized, version-controlled policy (defined in a simple allowlist schema) specifying which actions each agent may perform, what data scopes (e.g., PII, internal APIs) it can access, and which peers it can communicate with. Non-compliant requests are blocked in real time, and every decision—allow or deny—is recorded in an immutable, structured audit log for security review.

**Agent Permission Scopes**: a declarative manifest per agent specifying allowed tool calls, data stores, and peer communication targets, enforced at runtime by an orchestrator that intercepts and validates every action before execution.

**Step Budget Gate**  
A runtime enforcer predefines per-agent action limits on token consumption and latency. When any step exceeds its budget, it is immediately terminated and flagged for review, preventing cost overruns and tail-latency violations in multi-agent workflows.

answers: Transactional Revocation: on grant revocation, the system immediately interrupts the affected step, rolls back any incomplete tool calls using pre-registered compensating actions, and saves a resume point so the workflow can continue from a clean state once the issue is resolved.

Live Capability Downgrade: when a grant is revoked mid-run, the orchestrator immediately tightens the agent’s active capability token to the surviving scope rather than halting the step, allowing the in-flight operation to complete with reduced permissions if the action can adapt within the new constraints.

Revocation-Coupled Snapshot Rollback: upon live revocation, the system automatically reinstates the last known-good sandbox snapshot taken before the revoked grant was exercised, instantly discarding any in-flight state changes, and quarantines all output events or artifacts produced by the halted step to prevent downstream agents from consuming tainted data.

Revocation-Adaptive Restart: When a live revocation halts an in-flight agent step, the platform automatically derives a narrowed capability set (original grant minus revoked permissions) and, if the step can still execute with the remaining capabilities, resumes it immediately using a new scoped token reflecting that restricted set.

Cascading Revocation: on revoking a grant, halt the step directly using it and immediately cancel all other in‑flight steps that depend on that halted step's output, preventing orphaned token spend and compute waste.

Capability Circuit Breaker:  
Each agent runs with a time-bounded, scoped token that names exactly the tools and datasets it may touch; a runtime interceptor checks every call. On any out-of-scope attempt, it instantly halts the agent, invalidates the token, executes a pre-registered rollback script to undo the step’s side effects, and logs a full incident record for operators—who can then adjust and re-issue the token.

Explain-to-Enforce Loop: every capability grant is linked to a short natural-language justification authored by the team member. The team reviews the report to either refine the justification (better expressing task boundaries) or revoke excess capabilities, building a shared, evolving mental model of what agents should be allowed to do.

Declarative Scope Gate: a pre-execution check that statically verifies every agent's planned tool/dataset references against a team-curated allowlist, blocking the workflow before any token is issued if a step exceeds the declared scope.

answers: Shadow Diff Gate: a release gate that shadows live traffic through a candidate workflow, computes a structured diff of agent decisions, tool calls, and outputs against the running version, and blocks promotion until the team annotates the diff with whether the observed behaviour matches their expectation, turning every rollout into a documented learning loop.

Live Hypothesis Check: teams annotate each workflow deployment with structured behavioural expectations (e.g., “Coordinator will escalate to human when confidence < 0.7”), the tool continuously samples production traces and flags any run where the observed agent actions contradict a stated expectation, attaching the divergent trace, the linked git diff, and a lightweight prompt to capture what the team learned, turning each surprise into a shared mental-model update.

Agent Accountability Trail: an append-only, cryptographically signed log of every inter-agent message and tool invocation, with automatic detection of privilege escalation, credential sharing, and PII exposure, served through role-based access so security reviewers can trace misuse back to specific workflow stages without exposing raw sensitive data.

Spaced Debrief Queue: re-surfaces logged hypothesis-outcome mismatches at expanding intervals, prompting recall of root cause and lesson, then captures the refined explanation as a durable design rule.

Cost-Weighted Sampler: selects production runs with the highest token consumption or latency spikes, clusters them by agent interaction graph similarity, and surfaces the most costly deviation patterns for review, helping teams learn where workflows waste resources or behave unexpectedly under load.

Feature Name: **Conversation Graph Diff**  
Mechanism: A visual side-by-side comparison of the actual inter-agent message sequence graph from a production trace against an expected reference graph (automatically inferred from system prompts and past normal behavior). Nodes represent agent turns, edges represent reply relationships. Added, missing, or reordered messages are highlighted with color-coded diffs; semantic drift per message is annotated via embedding distance. Clicking a diff opens the full context, allowing developers to instantly understand where and why the workflow deviated, reducing investigation friction and building trust through transparent, explorable evidence of emergent agent behavior.

Feature Name: Interaction Graph Diff
